title: USS Gerald R. Ford to return after record 309-day Red Sea deployment
summary: The US Navy aircraft carrier USS Gerald R. Ford set the record for longest modern US carrier deployment at 309 days as of April 12, 2026 supporting operations against Iran in the Red Sea, with the ship and its 4,500 sailors expected to dock in Virginia around mid-May.
contents:
# USS Gerald R. Ford Returns from Record Deployment

## Overview
The US Navy aircraft carrier USS Gerald R. Ford (CVN-78) reached 309 days at sea on April 12, 2026, surpassing all modern US carrier deployments. The ship and its crew of approximately 4,500 sailors operated continuously in the Red Sea supporting US operations against Iran. The carrier and its strike group plan to dock in Virginia around mid-May.

## Operational Context
Secretary of Defense Pete Hegseth told Congress on April 9 that extending the deployment followed a tough decision-making process in consultation with the Navy. The extension reflected sustained operational demand from CENTCOM during the Iran maritime confrontation and a tight pipeline of replacement carriers. USS Dwight D. Eisenhower completed sea trials on April 24 following 15 months of maintenance, while USS Theodore Roosevelt conducts workup training in 3rd Fleet waters.

## Crew Welfare Concerns
Extended deployments place strain on sailors and family support networks. Navy leaders emphasize mental health resources, port-call rotations, and reunion preparation. ABC News and other broadcast outlets cover the homecoming alongside policy debate over carrier force generation and shipyard maintenance backlogs.

## Strategic Significance
The Ford deployment underscores how the Iran confrontation, the Houthi threat to Red Sea shipping, and the US blockade declared April 13 strain US carrier availability. Replacement options include Eisenhower returning to deployment-readiness status and Theodore Roosevelt completing pre-deployment training.
